q: Which kinematic equation relates velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and time — but does NOT include displacement?
explain: $v = v_0 + at$ has no $\Delta x$ term. This is the go-to equation when displacement isn't involved.

q: A rock is dropped from rest. How far has it fallen after 2 seconds? (Use $g = 9.8\,\text{m/s}^2$)
explain: $\Delta y = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \frac{1}{2}(9.8)(4) = 19.6\,\text{m}$. The $t^2$ means distance grows fast!

q: A car accelerates from $10\,\text{m/s}$ to $30\,\text{m/s}$ over a distance of $80\,\text{m}$. What is its acceleration?
explain: Use $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2a\Delta x$: $900 = 100 + 2a(80)$ → $a = 800/160 = 5.0\,\text{m/s}^2$. This equation is perfect when time is unknown.

q: A ball is thrown upward at $20\,\text{m/s}$. How high does it go above the throw point?
In $F = ma$, what exactly does $F$ represent?
opts: The largest force acting on the object
The net force (vector sum of all forces)
correct: The net force (vector sum of all forces)
explain: $F$ is the NET force — the vector sum of ALL forces. A common mistake is using just one force instead of the sum.

q: How does your weight on the Moon compare to your weight on Earth?
explain: Moon's $g \approx 1.6\,\text{m/s}^2$ vs Earth's $9.8\,\text{m/s}^2$. Ratio ≈ 1/6. Mass stays the same, weight changes.

q: A $5\,\text{kg}$ box sits on a ramp at $30°$. What is the normal force?
explain: $N = mg\cos\theta = 5 \times 9.8 \times \cos 30° = 49 \times 0.866 = 42.5\,\text{N}$. Don't use $mg$ directly — the ramp changes the normal force!

q: An elevator accelerates upward at $3\,\text{m/s}^2$. A $60\,\text{kg}$ person inside feels:
explain: $N = m(g+a) = 60(9.8+3) = 768\,\text{N}$ vs normal $588\,\text{N}$. Upward acceleration increases apparent weight.

q: If you double an object's speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
explain: $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. Double $v$ → $v^2$ becomes $4v^2$ → KE quadruples. This is why speeding is so dangerous.

q: What condition is required for conservation of mechanical energy ($KE_1 + PE_1 = KE_2 + PE_2$)?
opts: Object must be moving in a straight line
explain: Mechanical energy is only conserved when friction, air resistance, and other non-conservative forces are absent.

q: A spring ($k = 200\,\text{N/m}$) is compressed $0.1\,\text{m}$. How much potential energy is stored?
explain: $PE_s = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}(200)(0.01) = 1.0\,\text{J}$. Don't forget the $x^2$ — compressing 2x gives 4x the energy!

q: When is work done by a force negative?
opts: When force and displacement are perpendicular
explain: $W = Fd\cos\theta$. When $\theta = 180°$, $\cos\theta = -1$, so work is negative. Friction always does negative work on moving objects.

q: Is momentum a scalar or a vector quantity?
explain: $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$. Since velocity is a vector, momentum is also a vector — it has direction. This matters in collisions!

q: Why do airbags reduce injury in car crashes?
opts: They reduce the change in momentum
They increase the time of impact, reducing force
correct: They increase the time of impact, reducing force
explain: $F\Delta t = \Delta p$. The impulse ($\Delta p$) is the same with or without an airbag. But airbags increase $\Delta t$, so $F$ decreases. Same stop, less force.

q: In a perfectly elastic collision between equal masses, if one is at rest, what happens after collision?
opts: They stick together and move at half speed
The moving one stops; the resting one moves at the original speed
Both move at half the original speed
They bounce back at equal speeds
correct: The moving one stops; the resting one moves at the original speed
explain: Plug into $v_{1f} = \frac{(m_1-m_2)v_{1i} + 2m_2 v_{2i}}{m_1+m_2}$. With $m_1=m_2$ and $v_{2i}=0$: $v_{1f}=0$, $v_{2f}=v_{1i}$. They swap velocities!

q: What direction does centripetal acceleration always point?
opts: Toward the center of the circle
correct: Toward the center of the circle
explain: Centripetal means "center-seeking." It always points inward toward the center, perpendicular to velocity. It changes direction, not speed.

q: Is centripetal force a "new" type of force?
opts: No, it's the net inward force from existing forces
correct: No, it's the net inward force from existing forces
explain: $F_c$ is NOT a separate force. It's the name for whichever force (tension, friction, gravity, normal) provides the inward net force.

q: As a satellite's orbital radius increases, what happens to its orbital speed?
explain: $v = \sqrt{GM/r}$. Larger $r$ → smaller $v$. Counterintuitive but true: higher orbits are SLOWER. (Mass of satellite doesn't matter!)

q: In Newton's law of gravitation, what does $r$ represent?
explain: $r$ is the distance between the CENTERS of mass, not surfaces. For Earth-surface problems, $r = R_{\text{Earth}}$, not zero.

q: How does escape velocity compare to circular orbital velocity at the same radius?
explain: $v_e = \sqrt{2GM/r} = \sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{GM/r} = \sqrt{2}\,v_{\text{orbital}}$. Escape velocity is about 1.41× orbital velocity.

q: At an altitude of $400\,\text{km}$ above Earth, the gravitational field strength is approximately:
explain: $g = GM/r^2$ with $r = 6370 + 400 = 6770\,\text{km}$. $g \approx 8.7\,\text{m/s}^2$. The ISS is NOT in zero gravity — astronauts float because they're in free fall!

q: In $v = f\lambda$, what determines the wave speed?
explain: Wave speed depends on the MEDIUM (e.g., air, water, string). Changing the source frequency changes wavelength, not speed. This is a fundamental wave concept.

q: Which harmonics are possible in a pipe closed at one end?
explain: A closed pipe has a displacement node at the closed end and antinode at the open end. Only odd harmonics satisfy this: $f_n = \frac{nv}{4L}$, $n = 1,3,5,\ldots$

q: In the wave equation $y(x,t) = A\sin(kx - \omega t)$, what does the wave number $k$ equal?
explain: $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (radians per meter). Similarly $\omega = 2\pi f$ (radians per second). Both are spatial/temporal frequency measures.

q: What are the SI units of resistance?
explain: Resistance is measured in Ohms ($\Omega$). From $V = IR$: $\Omega = \text{V}/\text{A}$. Named after Georg Ohm.

q: In a series circuit with multiple resistors, what quantity is the same through all of them?
explain: In series: current is the same everywhere (only one path). Voltages add up. This is the opposite of parallel circuits.

q: Two $10\,\Omega$ resistors in parallel give a total resistance of:
explain: $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{10} = \frac{2}{10}$, so $R = 5\,\Omega$. Parallel resistance is always LESS than the smallest individual resistor.

q: For calculating power in a series circuit, which form of $P = IV = I^2R = V^2/R$ is most convenient?
explain: In series, current $I$ is the same through all components. So $P = I^2R$ lets you find each resistor's power directly. For parallel, use $P = V^2/R$ since voltage is the same.

q: At what angle between velocity $\vec{v}$ and magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is the magnetic force on a charge maximum?
explain: $F = qvB\sin\theta$. Maximum when $\sin\theta = 1$, i.e., $\theta = 90°$. At $0°$ or $180°$, the force is zero — the charge moves parallel to the field.

q: A positive charge moves to the right. The magnetic field points into the page. The force on the charge is:
explain: Use the right-hand rule: fingers point right (v), curl into the page (B) → thumb points downward. For negative charges, use left hand or reverse the result.

q: In the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, what units MUST temperature be in?
explain: $T$ MUST be in Kelvin. $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. Always convert: $K = °C + 273.15$. Using Celsius gives a nonsensical result.

q: A gas absorbs $400\,\text{J}$ of heat and does $150\,\text{J}$ of work. What is $\Delta U$?
explain: $\Delta U = Q - W = 400 - 150 = +250\,\text{J}$. Positive $Q$ = heat in, positive $W$ = work done by system. Internal energy increased.

q: What does $E = mc^2$ fundamentally tell us?
opts: Energy can be created from nothing
explain: Mass IS a form of energy. They're two sides of the same coin — convertible. A small mass yields enormous energy because $c^2 \approx 9 \times 10^{16}$.

q: Does a baseball (mass $\approx 0.15\,\text{kg}$, speed $\approx 30\,\text{m/s}$) have a de Broglie wavelength?
opts: No, only subatomic particles have wave properties
explain: ALL matter has wave properties! $\lambda = h/p = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}/(0.15 \times 30) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{m}$. It exists but is far too small to detect.

q: A spaceship travels at $v = 0.8c$. How much does time dilate for the astronauts compared to Earth?
explain: $t = \frac{t_0}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}} = \frac{t_0}{\sqrt{1 - 0.64}} = \frac{t_0}{0.6} = 1.67\,t_0$. For every 1 second on the ship, 1.67 seconds pass on Earth.
